entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: the word standing within the speaker's self

entities.name: brahman
entities.cite: language at its own root, which by 1.1 *is* the ultimate

entities.name: śabdāntar
entities.cite: the word stationed *within the speaker's self*: not the audible fourth part but the inner totality of speech

entities.name: mahāṛṣabha
entities.cite: the 'great bull' is the mahan rsabha of the old texts

entities.name: sāyujya
entities.cite: union, the technical term of Vedic soteriology (con-yoking, being joined-with), is what is sought

axioms.cite: language at its own root, which by 1.1 *is* the ultimate

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: the word stationed *within the speaker's self*

axioms.name: maharsabha
axioms.cite: The 'great bull' is the mahan rsabha of the old texts

axioms.name: isyate
axioms.cite: union, the technical term of Vedic soteriology (con-yoking, being joined-with), is what is sought

denials.cite: not the audible fourth part but the inner totality of speech

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: the word standing within the speaker's self

claims.name: isyate
claims.cite: union with which is what is sought

rejected_readings.label: audible_word_confusion
rejected_readings.rendering: The audible fourth part of speech standing within the speaker is declared to be the great bull.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly contrasts the inner word (sabdantar) with the audible fourth part: 'not the audible fourth part but the inner totality of speech'. This reading violates that essential ontological distinction and confuses two different levels of the word.

rejected_readings.label: external_deity_goal
rejected_readings.rendering: Union with an external deity is what is sought.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary states the goal is 'union with language at its own root', not with a transcendent deity outside language. This reading misplaces the soteriological object and misunderstands the non-dualistic character of the soteriology.

notes: The verse inaugurates the soteriology, locating the ultimate goal within language itself via the inner word (sabdantar). This grounds the system's later claim that grammar is the path to liberation (dvaram apavargasya, 1.14). Entities map to six sorts naturally: sabda and brahman are absolute; sabdantar is manifestation; atman and sayujya are powers; maharsabha is appellative property.
